qa_pairs.question: What High-Performance Computing (HPC) systems are available through the Alliance, and what are their primary characteristics or recommended uses?
qa_pairs.answer: The Alliance offers several systems: Arbutus, which is a cloud site allowing users to launch and customize virtual machines; Fir, Narval, Nibi, and Rorqual, which are general-purpose clusters (or supercomputers) composed of a variety of nodes, including large memory and GPU accelerators; and Trillium, a homogeneous cluster (or supercomputer) specifically designed for large parallel jobs exceeding 1000 cores. Users can request access to these systems via the CCDB portal.

qa_pairs.question: How do users obtain an account and log in to the Alliance's new national High-Performance Computing (HPC) systems?
qa_pairs.answer: Users must first apply for a CCDB account. The password to log in to all new national systems is the same one used for the CCDB, and the username will be displayed at the top of the CCDB page once a user has logged in.

qa_pairs.question: What types of information are helpful when discussing with technical support to determine the most suitable Alliance High-Performance Computing (HPC) resource for a particular task?
qa_pairs.answer: When seeking guidance, it is helpful to provide details such as the software intended for use (including whether it requires a commercial license, can be used non-interactively, and runs on Linux), and rough estimates for memory, time, computing power, accelerators, storage, and network bandwidth required by a typical job. Technical support is available to assist users in finding these answers if they are unsure.
